transcript: [patient] patient is julia jones , date of birth 5/16/1996 . she is a new breast reduction consult . hello , how are you doing today ? i'm dr. gonzalez , it's nice to meet you . nice to meet you ma'am , how are you ? i'm doing good , thank you . so you're here to discuss a breast reduction , correct ? yes , that's right . okay , perfect . let me just get logged in here . have you met with anyone before about a breast reduction ? no , i have n't . okay and how long have you been considering this ? it's probably been about six or 7 years but i mean it's always been in the back of my mind . [doctor] well , i performed a lot of breast reduction surgeries pretty much every week , so it is a very common procedure . [patient] okay , that's reassuring . yeah , so what i wan na do first is just get a little bit of history about the sort of symptoms that you're having and then i'll do a quick exam . okay . that sounds good . and then we will talk more about the surgery and what it entails and at this point who is your insurance provider ? actually my husband just accepted a new job and i will be on his plan which i believe is not going to be blue cross , but it may be changing . [doctor] okay . what we typically do when we do a breast reduction consult , [patient] document your symptoms , symptoms , we will take some pictures , then they get sent to the insurance company and what they will do is decide whether it's approved or medically necessary or not but we want to make sure we send it to the right insurance . do you know when you will be on the new plan ? mostly july . okay , so we may just wait until july to submit for approval at that time . okay . [doctor] so regarding your symptoms , do you experience back pain ? [patient] well , i've been going to the chiropractor for a few years . [doctor] pain here and here not painful.when i'm walking at my desk all day . [patient] okay and in your neck and your upper back and how long has this been going on would you say 5 years , 10 years or even since puberty ? i would say since i was in high school . okay and do you have the bra strap indentations in your shoulders i saw that you're wearing about a g cup . [doctor] patient regular wellbe therapy more comfortable and that is extensive than . [patient] yeah , i have heard that as well and have you had any children , are you planning to have any children in the future ? no , i do not have any kids now but we might plan to in the future . okay and at this time , are you experiencing any numbness or tingling in the , in the hands or any nipple pain ? sometimes , yes . okay and rashes , do you get any rashes underneath your breasts and , and it could be from sweating ? no , but they are always sweaty . okay and then do you feel like your activities are limited because of the size ? there are certain things i do when i have to hold them like working out and any jumping or running and it hurts . and it hurts , okay and , and you saw a chiropractor , but what about physical therapy for your back pain or do you take any pain medicine for it ? no , but i do take a migraine medication , propranolol . that's okay . so let's see , i know that you have a history of breast cancer in your family . have you had any mammograms and how old was your mother when she had it ? mom was 40 and they recommended me starting at age 30 for mammogram . i'm still , i mean i'm 25 , so i'm still a little bit young . [doctor] okay , and otherwise it seems like you're really healthy . [patient] yes for the most part . i lost about 10 pounds over the past few months and i still would like to lose about 20 more pounds . congratulations , that's great and then you're welcome and so do you use any tobacco , drugs or alcohol and then you said that you were going to school , what are you going to school for ? i drink a little wine on occasion and for school , i'm currently setting psychology . [doctor] okay , that's great . alright , so what we will do is we will go ahead and take a look . i'm going to take a couple of measurements and we will kinda talk about the surgery afterwards . so go ahead and stand up for me julia . [patient] okay , so looking at the measurements , it looks like one breast is a little lower than the other , yeah . okay . well , thank you so much . it looks like i have all the measurements that we need . you can go ahead and cover up now . okay . [doctor] so i think you are a great candidate for a breast reduction . when we talk about a breast reduction , what happens is that we remove the tissue out of the breast . [patient] okay , that's fine . and we would then lift them and elevate the nipple position in order to help with your back pain , neck pain and because i'm a plastic surgeon , of course i want them to look nice as well . wow . [doctor] so typically when you do the breast reduction , we make an incision around the nipple straight down and then underneath and it kinda looks like an anchor below . [patient] then straight down and underneath and through that incision , we're able to to lift the breast and we'll take off any extra fat and brush breast tissue , so that way it becomes smaller in size . and i noticed that you would like to be a b cup , so i do tell every patient , we can make you a small as your blood supply allows and what that means is we must move the nipple without cutting off any of it's blood supply during the surgery . okay . well , thank you for explaining that . you're welcome . and in your case , you'll probably be a small c a b cup might be a little bit small , but we'll see once i'm doing the , the procedure for you . okay . [doctor] and typically the surgery takes about 3 hours you will have drains one in each side and that helps prevent fluid from building up in the breast and that stays in for about a week and then we will remove them in the clinic . [patient] and you'll have a clear plastic tape over your incision that should help with the scars and we've seen that patients who have lighter skin , the scars will tend to be red at first and then it takes about a year for a scar to mature in line . okay . well i'm not too worried about the card . okay and i do like to keep patients overnight , it's just going to be for one night in the hospital , [doctor] and we just wan na make sure your pain is controlled , make sure you're not nauseated , all of that stuff . some patients wan na go home that same day , [patient] but you know that some people get nauseated and the last thing that i want is for you to be vomiting at home alone without the , the support here at the facility . okay . yeah , that sounds good . okay . and then about 30 % of patients say that they can not breast feed after a breast reduction and the reason is the breast is made up of fat and it's also made up of a gland and the gland is what produces the milk , okay . and so in order to reduce the size of the breast , we have to take out both the fat and the glands , so it just depends on how much is left , whether you can breast-feed in the future and then also the breast will change over time if you gain weight , they will get larger , things like that . yeah , okay . and if you have children they will change as well . they will enlarge especially if you can breast feed , but they will go back down will appear a little bit deflated . yeah i do n't know if i'm interested in breast feeding . okay the hormones of pregnancy though will change the breast , so that's just something to be aware of and i tell all younger patients that . okay , yeah , absolutely . and then typically nipple sensation is fine after a breast reduction , but there is a slight chance that you will have an alteration in the nipple sensation or not have any sensation in the nipple , generally the nipple is just fine unless it is a massive reduction and it's quite normal after surgery to be a little bit bruised and then it just takes some time for that to go away . yeah , okay . but other than that you know most patients are very happy after the breast reduction because of the symptoms of the back pain , the neck pain , you can feel relief almost immediately . so do you have any questions julia about the process or anything like that ? no , not really . [doctor] okay and , and i do think you are a good candidate for it and i think you will benefit from it as well . [patient] good . i look forward to the release . i think you just have to do it when you are ready when you know that you feel like it's a good time because it is a commitment and you will have some activity restrictions for about 6 weeks after surgery and no heavy lifting and i do say no driving for 2 to 3 weeks and the drain stay in like i said for about a week . okay well i am ready as soon as my insurance is switched over . okay . well i think from this point if you want to , we can take photos today while you're here . i have all the documentation now and the note about your symptoms and that's what the insurance company is going to look for , so thank you for answering those questions . so once we have the new provider , we'll go ahead and get that submitted and then when they approve it , what the office will do is they'll work with you on a surgery date and then we'll meet again right before the surgery to answer any last minute questions . [doctor] and go over the instructions in more detail and things like that . [patient] okay . well , i will notify the office as soon as i have the insurance information . that sounds great and then we can get it all in process and it was so nice to meet you and catherine will be right in to get your photos . julia jones is a 25-year-old female with symptomatic macromycinia presenting for evaluation of breast reduction . the patient has attempted nonsurgical treatments with minimal relief and is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty . her minimum required resection based on a bsa of 2.65 with the schnur scale is 1792 g which should be attainable . given her extremely large breasts and the measurements above , there is potential for using a free nipple graft . physical exam findings of breast . inspection reveals asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally . [doctor] there is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally . no breast tenderness , masses or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable . [patient] julia jones is a 25-year-old female with symptomatic macromastia presenting for evaluation of breast reduction . the patient has attempted nonsurgical treatments with minimal relief and is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction and mammoplasty . her minimum required resection based on a bsa of 2.65 , [doctor] with the schnur scale is 1792 g which should be attainable . given her extremely large breasts and the measurements above , there is potential for using a free nipple graft . physical exam findings of breast . inspection reveals asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally . there is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally . no breast tenderness , masses or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Macromastia.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Julia Jones is a 25-year-old female who presents for evaluation of macromastia.

Ms. Jones reports this is her first visit in regard to a breast reduction and has been interested in getting a breast reduction for approximately 7 years. Currently she has Blue Cross Blue Shield insurance but will be added to her spouse's insurance plan in 07/2021.

Current breast size is a G cup, although she primarily wears a 34 E sports bra for comfort, and desired breast size is a B or C cup. No prior pregnancies, potentially planning on pregnancies in the future, and unsure of breast feeding.

The patient endorses back and neck pain since she was in high school. Her back and neck pain has been managed by a chiropractor for a few years. She reports weight loss of 10 pounds over the past few months and would like to lose an additional 20 pounds.

She endorses the following symptoms because of her macromastia for 7 years:

Chronic upper back pain: Yes.
Bra strap grooving in shoulders or indentation on lateral chest wall: Occasionally.
Neck pain: Yes.
Nipple pain or numbness: Yes.
Paresthesia of the hands or arms: Yes.
Intertrigo, rash or yeast or other skin problems beneath breasts: Yes, persistent sweating.
Stooped posture: Yes.
Physical activity limited by breast size: Yes.

The patient has undergone the following therapies:
Physical therapy: No.

Special bras: Yes.
Non-narcotic pain medication: No.
Narcotic pain medication: No.

Her previous breast surgery includes:
Previous reconstruction: No.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Significant for migraine headaches.

FAMILY HISTORY

Mother with prior history of breast cancer at age 40.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Tobacco: No prior history.
Alcohol: Socially. One glass per week.
Drug: No prior history.
Occupational and Educational: Currently studying to be a psychologist.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Propranolol for migraine prevention.

VITALS

BSA 2.65

PHYSICAL EXAM

Breasts
Inspection reveals asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally. There is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally. No breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable.

ASSESSMENT

 Macromastia.

Julia Jones is a 25-year-old female with symptomatic macromastia presenting for evaluation of breast reduction. The patient has attempted non-surgical treatments with minimal relief and is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty. Her minimum required resection based on a BSA of 2.65 with the Schnur scale is 1792 g, which should be attainable. Given her extremely large breasts and the measurements above, there is potential for using a free nipple graft.

PLAN

An extensive discussion of breast reduction surgery was performed with the patient with consideration of the patient's age, co-morbidities, previous surgical history, body habitus, BMI, and smoking history. Surgical time, immediate recovery course, and longer term recovery course were discussed. Relief of the patient's current symptoms related to macromastia was not guaranteed though is expected to improve. Specific breast size was discussed, also not guaranteed. I explained the expected outcomes of using a free nipple graft such as an insensate nipple and an inability to breast feed. The patient understands that the decision for a free nipple graft will be made intraoperatively based on assessment of nipple perfusion.

Risks and benefits of were discussed with the patient The patient understands the above risks, benefits and alternatives to surgery and wishes to proceed with bilateral reduction mammaplasty.

The patient will be switching to a different insurance policy in 07/2021. Once we get the new insurance information from the patient, we will submit it to her insurance company. If approved, she will return for preoperative counseling and consent.

Return to clinic prior to scheduled surgery date for preoperative counseling and consent.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Julia Jones, a 25-year-old female, presents for evaluation of breast reduction due to symptomatic macromastia. She has been experiencing back pain, neck pain, and discomfort due to her breast size, which has been a concern for about 7 years. She has tried nonsurgical treatments, including chiropractic care, with minimal relief. She is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty. She is currently a student, studying psychology, and has a family history of breast cancer, with her mother diagnosed at age 40. She is otherwise healthy, having lost 10 pounds in the past few months and plans to lose an additional 20 pounds.

Past medical history: The patient has a family history of breast cancer, with her mother diagnosed at age 40. She has not had any mammograms, but plans to start at age 30. She does not have any known allergies.

Review of systems: The patient reports back pain, neck pain, and discomfort due to her breast size, which improves with bra use. She experiences numbness and tingling in her hands and occasional nipple pain. She has not experienced any rashes or discharge.

Current medications: She takes propranolol for migraines.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: Inspection reveals asymmetrical breasts with severe ptosis bilaterally. There is no nipple retraction or discharge bilaterally. No breast tenderness, masses, or axillary lymphadenopathy is palpable.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Julia Jones is a 25-year-old female with symptomatic macromastia, ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty.
Problem list: Macromastia; Active.

Plan:
I prescribed no medications. I will submit the documentation and photos to the insurance company for approval. Once approved, we will schedule the surgery and meet again to discuss the procedure and answer any questions. The patient will need to have a clear plastic tape over the incision, which may be red initially but will mature in about a year. The patient will have drains in each breast for about a week and will need to avoid heavy lifting, driving, and strenuous activities for 2-3 weeks. There is a 30% chance of reduced nipple sensation or no sensation after the surgery.

Follow-up: The patient will need to follow up with the office after the insurance approval and before the surgery to review the instructions and any last-minute questions.

She has been experiencing back and neck pain for approximately 10 years, which has been exacerbated by her large breast size. She has tried nonsurgical treatments with minimal relief. She is an ideal candidate for bilateral reduction mammoplasty. The patient has a history of breast cancer in her family, with her mother diagnosed at 40 years old, and she has been recommended to start mammograms at age 30. She is currently 25 and has not had any mammograms yet. She has also been experiencing numbness and tingling in her hands and nipple pain, as well as rashes under her breasts, which she attributes to sweating. She has been taking a migraine medication, propranolol, for her migraines. She is planning to start taking her husband's insurance, Blue Cross, in July. She is a student studying psychology and occasionally drinks wine. She is not a smoker and does not use illicit substances.

I will meet with the patient again before the surgery to answer any last-minute questions and review instructions in more detail. The patient will be admitted to the hospital for one night for pain control and to ensure she is not nauseated. She will have drains in each breast for approximately one week, and she will need to avoid heavy lifting and driving for 2-3 weeks post-operatively.
